I mean if I was doing a mailing for a self-esteem product right now and I could get it in the mail right now, there's no doubt in my mind I talk about Tonya Harding. And I talk about how this whole woman, all her problems and everything she's now going to face traces to a self-esteem issue. And I talk about Tonya Harding, why? Because everybody is focused on Tonya Harding. You can't turn the damn television set on, you can't pick up a tabloid, you can't go in a restaurant and over here a conversation and they're talking about Tonya Harding. Well, if I'm linked to what's already in their minds, I've got a whole their interest edge in selling financial stuff. I mean Jeff's got all his financial planners, all their advertising this year, all the anti-coin advertisers, the Clinton tax plan and the Clinton healthcare plan. Well, it's an obvious system goes on. Anybody's got any money, that's what's on their mind is what Clinton's doing to them. So you link to that and you give yourself an edge up. Seasonal stuff. People start thinking about major holidays a month out, a Christmas two months out. So if you're doing any marketing in October, you might as well, if there's any way to get Christmas into the store, you might as well put it in. Because that's their plan and their Christmas parties and they're thinking about their relatives coming and where they're going to go and how am I going to get this day off work and I mean that's what's going on here. So you might as well hook to it rather than fight it. 15 and 16 go together. How do you know? I don't understand the media. Tell me more about what you're talking about. You do this thing. Oh, like to go with a credit card. Well, first of all, you do have a fair amount of space. Most people don't put as much copy on those things as they could just like a ballot.ARY you're riding on the quiet endorsement of the package. So, if I'm going to sell Pearl Jewelry direct to the customer, and I'm going to do it with an Insurgent American Express statement stuffer, or even Markets' statement stuffer, I don't need to hear as much copy about who the appraisers are, and how the things are valued, and how the company's been a business for 300 years and all that, because I got American Express as a quiet endorsement, the American Express card holder has a certain level of confidence in anything advertised in that statement. So, that's a place that I can cut my story down. I'm still going to cover the face, but I can do it in one sense, and I can sacrifice. But, I don't want to make the mistake, for example, that, like in that story, for the use of Pearl's story, you might assume that people know there are both natural and artificial pearls. I'm not going to make that mistake. I'm going to tell them that there are both artificial and natural pearls, and that there's a huge difference in both appearance and price. And if you go to a jewelry store to compare a price, you've got to make sure you compare a natural to natural, not natural to artificial. I'm not going to make the mistake of assuming that they know there's a difference between solid gold jewelry and gold jewelry in which the name solid, the word solid, is not a thing. I'm going to cover that thing. Because if I start to assume that kind of knowledge, I'm in trouble. Does that make sense to you? Yes. And, you know, certain media only lend themselves to certain kind of offers. I mean, there's certain things you can do in statement stuffers, and certain things you can't. In broadcasts, there's certain things you can do on a spot, and certain things you just cannot sell in a minute. And, you know, all media is not right for all things. And that's one of the things like the Vegas Conference. Were you there for the whole conference? Yeah, I said it when I spoke. And the way everybody should be selling everything. Well, that just can't be. I mean, it is wrong. There's certain things that certain media work for, and certain media don't work for. There's some stuff you just can't sell in the store. And there's some stuff you just can't sell in Money Magazine. And there's some stuff you can't sell in an infomercial, but you can sell it in the store. I mean, you know, and you have to find what fits with each product. So copy alone is not the answer. It's a good idea to use a certain amount of very familiar terminology. But if you overdo it, you wipe out the effectiveness. The same thing is true with technical terminology, what I would call jargon. You want enough so that they know you know, but you don't want to overdo it, because it becomes transparently manipulative, almost like the letter where you drop in the guy's name, and they drop it in 6,000 times. But as a general rule of thumb, the more copies and or the more pages, not only the higher the response percentage, but the higher the price point, and the better, the more valuable the customer is after they're acquired, which brings us back to a conversation you and I would have at the very beginning about the difference between the person who responds to a classifying ad, the person who responds to a small display, and the person who responds to a full page ad. The person who reads in response to this ad is a better customer than a person who would give us 20 bucks from an ad this big. Even if we could get the 20 bucks, well, because he was more interested to start with, his decision to respond was better informed, more thoughtful, less impulsive, he was more involved in the process, and so we have a better customer. Now, that's my theory. The theory is less important than the reality. You know, I always tell people, there's a lot of things that are true that I don't know why they're true, and I do know experientially that they are true. I think you take the example through 1500 words, because at the end, the guy who, you know, did a message to this, or rather, he's thinking along the way, he's more, jumped out of it early on before they hit to the end, but you get them to the end of these long copy ads, you got a better quality... Or long letters, or... Long letters, whatever, but they get them to the end and they send to check. I have a response to the TV interview on the Italian Central Geographic. Very much so. There's an enormous difference between now and what you can sell on spot versus a half an hour, but the nature of the customer you get from a spot versus a half an hour. In terms of what you can outsell... Our people can see, saying that it's not fact, and they can never stay in the open. Yeah, but ultimately, they've seen a large part of it before the order, because they watch, you know, repetitively. I will tell you this, for example. In U.S. Gold's case, the old lead generation method was an ad in all these magazines, and it was not a copy-intensive ad. It was a pictorial ad. The lead from television, from the half-hour television show, is dramatically more valuable than the lead from the print ads. Because they watched it not all, some portion of that half-hour, watched another chunk of it three nights later, and responded, they saw it met the owner of the company, they saw real people who are making money, they saw people at the flea market buying the stuff. A lot of things happen that can't happen in that ad. And so that lead, if it costs us more, it doesn't cost less, but even if it costs more, the TV lead would be better than the print lead. You think you're positioning when the customer is different, if they've been exposed to a longer instance in our cell phone? Yeah, I think the whole relationship's different. And I think the person is different, to start with. I think the person who will lead a full-page ad is different than the person, again, who's in the classifieds, or who will respond to a small ad. My bet is that the guys who are responding to these, who hit the classifieds, but let's take these, are more looking at the words, part of the expression, and the guys who responded to all of them, than the guy who went through this magazine and only responded to one or two ads, and really read the ad. And a guy who's a quick hitter isn't going to have the temperaments that they're going to leave that whole thing. So, for any number of reasons, you're getting a different customer. And the upsell and continuing relationship value of that customer is different.
